text: Literary critic Alberto Manguel has observed that the history of silent reading is surprisingly short: for most of Western antiquity, reading was performed aloud, even when alone. The shift to silent reading, largely complete by the tenth century, was a cognitive revolution. It allowed readers to move at their own pace, pause without interrupting a performance, and re-read at will — transforming the text from a public event into a private experience of sustained interior thought. This privatization of reading created, for the first time, a portable space of contemplation that could be entered and exited by an individual at will.
question: What cognitive change did the shift to silent reading enable, according to Manguel?

options.text: It allowed the visual cortex to process language faster.
It created the first written grammars for vernacular languages.
It allowed readers to move at their own pace, pause, and re-read, turning text into private contemplation.
It reduced the social function of literature in favor of scholarly analysis.
